content.body: Organic reaction mechanisms describe the step-by-step movement of electrons. The main types are substitution (SN1, SN2), elimination, addition, and oxidation/reduction.

SN2: One step, backside attack, inversion of configuration
    Rate = k[substrate][nucleophile]
    Favoured: primary substrates, strong nucleophiles

SN1: Two steps, forms carbocation, racemisation
    Rate = k[substrate]
    Favoured: tertiary substrates, polar solvents

Elimination (E2): Removes HX across adjacent carbons → alkene

content.title: SN2 vs SN1
content.body:
CH₃Br + OH⁻ → CH₃OH + Br⁻ (SN2)
- Primary, proceeds in one step with inversion

(CH₃)₃CBr + H₂O → (CH₃)₃COH + HBr (SN1)
- Tertiary, forms stable 3° carbocation first, then attack

questions.question: SN2 reactions are favoured by:
questions.explanation: SN2 needs access to the carbon (primary is least hindered) and a strong nucleophile for attack

questions.question: A carbocation intermediate is formed in which mechanism?
questions.explanation: SN1 proceeds via a carbocation intermediate in the rate-determining step

questions.question: Elimination reactions produce:
questions.explanation: Elimination removes H and X from adjacent carbons, forming a C=C double bond (alkene)

questions.question: What makes a good nucleophile?
questions.explanation: Nucleophiles donate electrons to electrophilic carbon — they must be electron-rich

questions.question: The rate of SN1 depends on:
questions.explanation: SN1 rate = k[substrate]. The nucleophile is not in the rate-determining step

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

content.body: Organic reaction mechanisms describe the step-by-step process by which reactants transform into products. Understanding mechanisms allows prediction of products and reaction conditions.

content.title: Types of Mechanisms
content.body:
Nucleophilic substitution (SN):
SN2 — one step, backside attack, inversion of configuration, favoured by primary halogenoalkanes
SN1 — two step, carbocation intermediate, favoured by tertiary halogenoalkanes

Electrophilic addition (EA):
Alkenes + Br₂ → dibromoalkane
Br₂ acts as electrophile, attacks π bond

Electrophilic substitution (ES):
Benzene + Br₂/FeBr₃ → bromobenzene
Aromatics prefer substitution to keep ring intact

Curly arrow conventions:
→ represents movement of an electron pair
Arrow from electron-rich to electron-poor
Homolytic fission: each atom gets one electron (free radical)
Heterolytic fission: one atom gets both electrons (ions formed)

questions.question: SN2 mechanism is favoured by...
questions.explanation: SN2 requires backside attack. Primary halogenoalkanes have less steric hindrance, favouring SN2.

questions.question: In SN1, a carbocation intermediate forms. Most stable carbocation is...
questions.explanation: Tertiary carbocations are most stable because three alkyl groups donate electrons by induction, stabilising the positive charge.

questions.question: Electrophilic addition occurs with...
questions.explanation: Alkenes have a C=C double bond that acts as an electron donor. Electrophiles add across the double bond.

questions.question: Benzene undergoes electrophilic substitution rather than addition because...
options: Substitution preserves the stable aromatic ring
questions.answer: Substitution preserves the stable aromatic ring
questions.explanation: Benzene's ring has 6 delocalized electrons giving special stability. Substitution maintains this aromatic stability.

questions.question: A curly arrow in a mechanism represents...
options: Movement of a pair of electrons
questions.answer: Movement of a pair of electrons
questions.explanation: Curly arrows show the movement of electron pairs during bond making and breaking.

questions.question: Homolytic fission produces...
questions.explanation: Homolytic fission splits a bond so each atom gets one electron, forming two free radicals (species with unpaired electrons).

questions.question: In electrophilic addition of HBr to propene, Markovnikov's rule predicts Br attaches to...
questions.explanation: Markovnikov's rule: the electrophile (H⁺) adds to the carbon with more H atoms, and nucleophile (Br⁻) to the other (more substituted).

questions.question: SN2 results in...
questions.explanation: SN2 involves backside attack — the nucleophile attacks from the opposite side to the leaving group, inverting the stereochemistry (Walden inversion).

questions.question: A nucleophile is...
questions.explanation: Nucleophiles are electron-rich (have lone pairs or negative charge) and attack electron-poor carbon atoms.

questions.question: FeBr₃ acts as a catalyst in bromination of benzene because it...
options: Acts as Lewis acid to activate Br₂
questions.answer: Acts as Lewis acid to activate Br₂
questions.explanation: FeBr₃ is a Lewis acid that accepts electrons from Br₂, polarising it to form an electrophile that can attack benzene.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Carbonyl compounds contain the C=O functional group. Aldehydes have the carbonyl at the end of a chain (−CHO), ketones have it in the middle (C=O). Carboxylic acids contain −COOH.

content.title: Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
content.body:
Nucleophilic addition (with HCN):
CHO + HCN → CH(OH)CN (hydroxynitrile)

Reduction:
Aldehyde + [H] → primary alcohol
Ketone + [H] → secondary alcohol
(using NaBH₄ or LiAlH₄)

Oxidation:
Aldehyde → carboxylic acid (oxidised easily)
Ketone — cannot be oxidised (no H on carbonyl C)

content.title: Tests to Distinguish Aldehydes from Ketones
content.body:
Tollens' reagent (silver mirror test): aldehydes reduce Ag⁺ to Ag mirror, ketones don't
Fehling's solution: aldehydes give brick-red precipitate, ketones don't
Benedict's test: similar to Fehling's

Carboxylic acids:
• Weak acids, react with carbonates: RCOOH + Na₂CO₃ → CO₂ gas
• Esterification: RCOOH + R'OH ⇌ RCOOR' + H₂O

questions.question: What functional group do aldehydes contain?
questions.explanation: Aldehydes have the −CHO group at the end of the carbon chain.

questions.question: Ketones differ from aldehydes because ketones...
options: Have C=O in middle of chain
questions.answer: Have C=O in middle of chain
questions.explanation: In ketones, the carbonyl group C=O is flanked by two carbon groups (not at the end of the chain).

questions.question: Tollens' reagent distinguishes aldehydes from ketones by...
questions.explanation: Aldehydes reduce the silver ions in Tollens' reagent to silver metal, forming a mirror. Ketones cannot be oxidised this way.

questions.question: Reduction of an aldehyde gives...
questions.explanation: Reducing an aldehyde (−CHO) adds H₂ to give a primary alcohol (−CH₂OH).

questions.question: Reduction of a ketone gives...
questions.explanation: Reducing a ketone (−CO−) gives a secondary alcohol (−CHOH−).

questions.question: Esterification is a reaction between...
questions.explanation: Esterification: RCOOH + R'OH ⇌ RCOOR' + H₂O (reversible, needs acid catalyst H₂SO₄)

questions.question: Carboxylic acids react with carbonates to produce...
questions.explanation: RCOOH + Na₂CO₃ → RCOONa + H₂O + CO₂. The CO₂ causes fizzing — a test for carboxylic acids.

questions.question: NaBH₄ is used as a...
questions.explanation: Sodium borohydride (NaBH₄) is a mild reducing agent used to reduce aldehydes and ketones to alcohols.

questions.question: Why can't ketones be oxidised by Tollens' reagent?
options: No H atom on the carbonyl carbon
questions.answer: No H atom on the carbonyl carbon
questions.explanation: Oxidation of carbonyl compounds requires an H on the carbonyl carbon (as in aldehydes). Ketones have no such H, so cannot be oxidised.

questions.question: The ester formed from ethanoic acid and methanol is...
questions.explanation: Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH) + methanol (CH₃OH) → methyl ethanoate (CH₃COOCH₃) + water.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Benzene C₆H₆: planar hexagonal ring
Delocalised π electrons (resonance stabilisation)
Not 3 separate double bonds — all C-C bonds equal

Electrophilic substitution (not addition):
Preserves aromatic ring stability
1. Nitration: C₆H₆ + HNO₃ → C₆H₅NO₂ + H₂O (H₂SO₄, 50°C)
2. Halogenation: C₆H₆ + Cl₂ → C₆H₅Cl + HCl (AlCl₃ catalyst)
3. Friedel-Crafts alkylation: C₆H₆ + CH₃Cl → C₆H₅CH₃ + HCl (AlCl₃)
4. Sulphonation: C₆H₆ + H₂SO₄(fuming) → C₆H₅SO₃H

content.title: Why Substitution not Addition?
content.body:
Addition would break aromaticity (lose ~150 kJ/mol stabilisation)
EAS mechanism:
1. Electrophile attacks ring, forming arenium ion (+)
2. H⁺ removed, restoring aromaticity
3. Substituted benzene + HX produced

content.title: Phenol and Aniline
content.body:
Phenol C₆H₅OH: more reactive than benzene
→ tribromo product with Br₂(aq) (no catalyst needed!)

Aniline C₆H₅NH₂: made by reducing nitrobenzene
→ used in dye manufacture
→ very reactive: produces tribromo product easily

questions.question: Benzene is unusually stable because
options: delocalised π electrons form stable aromatic system
questions.answer: delocalised π electrons form stable aromatic system
questions.explanation: Aromatic stabilisation energy ~150 kJ/mol from delocalised π system over entire ring

questions.question: Benzene undergoes substitution not addition to
questions.explanation: Addition would break π delocalisation destroying aromaticity. Substitution maintains ring stability

questions.question: Nitration of benzene uses
questions.explanation: Nitration: mixed acid (HNO₃+H₂SO₄). H₂SO₄ protonates HNO₃ → NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion, the electrophile)

questions.question: Friedel-Crafts acylation produces
questions.explanation: Friedel-Crafts acylation: ArH + RCOCl → ArCOR + HCl (AlCl₃ catalyst). Ketone product

questions.question: Phenol more reactive than benzene in EAS because
options: OH group donates electrons into ring by resonance, activating it
questions.answer: OH group donates electrons into ring by resonance, activating it
questions.explanation: OH lone pairs delocalize into ring → increased electron density → more reactive toward electrophiles

questions.question: Nitrobenzene reduced to give
questions.explanation: C₆H₅NO₂ + 6[H] → C₆H₅NH₂ + 2H₂O (Sn/HCl reduction). Aniline used in dye industry

questions.question: All C-C bonds in benzene are equal length because
options: there is resonance — bonds intermediate between single and double
questions.answer: there is resonance — bonds intermediate between single and double
questions.explanation: Delocalisation: all bonds are 140pm (between single 154pm and double 134pm)

questions.question: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid catalyst (AlCl₃) but phenol reacts with Br₂ without catalyst because
options: phenol's -OH activates ring sufficiently for Br₂ to react directly
questions.answer: phenol's -OH activates ring sufficiently for Br₂ to react directly
questions.explanation: Phenol ring is so electron-rich that electrophilic substitution with Br₂ occurs without catalyst, giving 2,4,6-tribromophenol

questions.question: Mechanism of electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): first step is
options: electrophile attacks π ring forming carbocation (arenium ion)
questions.answer: electrophile attacks π ring forming carbocation (arenium ion)
questions.explanation: EAS: E⁺ attacks electron-rich ring → arenium ion (sigma complex) → lose H⁺ to restore aromaticity

questions.question: Industrial importance of benzene chemistry
options: basis of petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals, dyes, plastics: billions of tonnes of chemicals derived from benzene
questions.answer: basis of petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals, dyes, plastics: billions of tonnes of chemicals derived from benzene
questions.explanation: Benzene is a key petrochemical feedstock: styrene, phenol, aniline, aspirin, paracetamol all derived from benzene

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Advanced Organic Chemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Advanced Organic Chemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Advanced Organic Chemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Advanced Organic Chemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Advanced Organic Chemistry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Advanced Organic Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Advanced Organic Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
